panels.title: Raw RTT vs EWMA RTT (primary + active wire)
Active Wire (Sticky Fallback)
panels.description: Sticky-fallback active-wire state per uplink+transport. `active wire idx` 0 = primary (operator's first-choice transport); non-zero = fallback (`fallbacks[idx-1]` of `[[outline.uplinks.fallbacks]]`). `pin remaining` is seconds until auto-failback snaps active back to primary. `fallbacks` is the configured count for context. Only uplinks with at least one fallback declared appear here.

Effective browser-fingerprint diversification strategy by uplink count. `strategy` label is one of `none`, `per_host_stable`, `random` and reflects the per-uplink override when set, otherwise the process-wide default from `--fingerprint-profile` / top-level `fingerprint_profile`. Each cell shows how many uplinks in the selected group(s) currently sit on that strategy. If the binary was launched without `init_fingerprint_profile_strategy` the entire population reads `none`.
panels.title: Fingerprint Strategy

panels.description: Live `bypass_when_down` state per group/transport: DIRECT means the group has no healthy uplink of that transport and new flows are escaping the tunnel via the direct path. Series exist only for groups with `bypass_when_down = true`; no data means the option is off everywhere.
panels.title: Direct Bypass (bypass_when_down)

panels.description: History of the group-level tunnel bypass: 1 = the group had no healthy uplink of that transport and new flows were dispatched DIRECT, 0 = traffic tunnelled normally. TUN flows bypass only while both transports of the group read 1.
panels.title: Direct Bypass Timeline

panels.description: Pinned-relay mid-session retry attempts, broken down by outcome. `success` — uplink (v1) and downlink (v2) replay completed without truncation. `failed_redial` — the redial itself or the v1/v2 control-frame consume timed out. `failed_replay` — the v1 uplink replay write failed mid-suffix. `buffer_overflow` — the client's v1 ring buffer overflowed (only seen under `tcp_mid_session_retry_overflow_policy = "soft"`). `downlink_truncated` — v2 was engaged and the server returned `REPLAY_TRUNCATED`. Stacked so the success ratio is visible at a glance.
panels.title: Retry Rate by Outcome

panels.description: Bytes/sec the client flushed to its SOCKS5 client out of the v2 `ORDR` replay payload, before fresh-upstream traffic resumed. Non-zero means at least one mid-session retry actually exercised the v2 Symmetric Downlink Replay path (not just negotiated it). Pulled from the existing transport bytes counter via the `direction="downlink_replay"` label.
panels.title: v2 Downlink Replay Throughput

panels.description: Live count of upstream transports still attached to an uplink that is no longer the active one for its (group, transport) — i.e. sessions that survived a Global / PerUplink switchover and continue to consume the old uplink's resources until they finish naturally. Non-zero is expected briefly after a switch (TCP cannot migrate); a value that fails to drain over time is the leak signal. Built by subtracting the active-uplink indicator from the open-connection gauge: any uplink with `global_active_uplink_info != 1` (or `per_uplink_active_uplink_info != 1` for the matching transport) but with open connections counts here.
panels.title: Stranded Connections on Inactive Uplinks (now)

panels.description: Currently open upstream transports per uplink, split by `transport` (tcp/udp). After a Global / PerUplink switchover the new active uplink starts climbing while the old one continues to drain — that drain rate is the recovery speed. Cross-check with `outline_ws_global_active_uplink_info` to identify which series belongs to the active vs the stranded uplink.
panels.title: Open Connections by Uplink

panels.description: Rate of upstream-transport closes on uplinks that were no longer active at close time, broken down by uplink. Spikes after a switchover are normal (stranded TCP sessions finishing naturally); a sustained non-zero rate without a recent switch suggests sessions are being dialled through a non-active uplink — typically a stale sticky route, a warm-standby pool that pre-dates the switch, or a `PerUplink`/`Global` mode misconfiguration. Pairs with the gauge above: integral of this rate ≈ delta of stranded gauge between scrapes.
panels.title: Inactive Uplink Close Rate (Leak)

panels.description: Operator-triggered cluster soft-switch migrations, by outcome. Only fires once an operator soft switch is in effect for a `shared_resume` (cluster) group. `migrated` = the session moved to the new edge cross-node (resume redial + replay succeeded); `redial_failed` = the migration redial failed so the session falls back to an RST teardown and reconnects; `not_ws_family` = the new active uplink cannot present the group resume id (not SS/VLESS-over-WS); `no_candidate` = the new active index had no dial candidate; `same_uplink` = the switch target equals the session's current uplink. A healthy switch is dominated by `migrated`; a spike in the others means sessions are being reset rather than migrated.
panels.title: Cluster Soft-switch Migrations by Outcome

panels.description: Resume-cache lookups when dialing an uplink wire, by transport, scope and result. `scope=group` means the resume id is shared across the group's edges (cluster `shared_resume`) so a `hit` is a cross-node-capable resume presented to whichever edge the wire landed on; `scope=uplink` is the standalone per-uplink resume. `result=hit` = a stored Session ID was presented (a resume attempt, i.e. session continuity across an edge switch); `miss` = a fresh session. On a cluster, a healthy migration shows `scope=group,result=hit` tracking the soft-switch rate.
panels.title: Cluster Resume Lookups (hit/miss)

targets.expr: label_replace((sum by (instance, uplink, transport) (rate(outline_ws_probe_runs_total{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group",probe!="standby_ws",result="error"}[$__rate_interval])) / clamp_min(sum by (instance, uplink, transport) (rate(outline_ws_probe_runs_total{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group",probe!="standby_ws"}[$__rate_interval])), 1e-9)), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

label_replace((sum by (instance, uplink, transport) (rate(outline_ws_probe_runs_total{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group",probe="standby_ws",result="error"}[$__rate_interval])) / clamp_min(sum by (instance, uplink, transport) (rate(outline_ws_probe_runs_total{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group",probe="standby_ws"}[$__rate_interval])), 1e-9)), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

panels.description: Per-state breakdown of TCP sockets owned by the process, parsed from /proc/self/net/tcp(6) intersected with /proc/self/fd. Stacked so CLOSE_WAIT/FIN_WAIT2/TIME_WAIT growth shows up against ESTABLISHED.
panels.title: Sockets by Kernel State (TCP)

panels.description: Net opened-minus-closed rate of upstream transports per source/protocol. Persistently positive lines indicate the leak source. Computed as rate(opened) - rate(closed); zero means open/close are balanced.

targets.expr: label_replace((sum by (instance, source, protocol) (rate(outline_ws_upstream_transports_total{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",result="opened"}[$__rate_interval])) - sum by (instance, source, protocol) (rate(outline_ws_upstream_transports_total{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",result="closed"}[$__rate_interval]))), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

panels.title: Upstream Transport Net Open Rate (Leak)
TCP Carrier Migration
panels.description: Transparent TUN TCP carrier migration on carrier death. carrier_migrated = a flow re-attached its parked upstream on a fresh carrier (app saw no reset). _miss = server did not confirm the resume (fell back to teardown). _dial_failed / _replay_failed = redial or byte-replay failed. replay_ring_overflow = a flow sent a chunk larger than its 64 KiB replay ring and is no longer resumable. Healthy: migrated rising, the failure series near zero.

panels.title: TCP Flow Events
panels.description: TUN TCP state-machine events: flow_created (new flows), window_stall (receive window hit zero), zero_window_probe (probes sent to reopen it), deferred_fin_sent (server FIN held until buffered data drained).

Downlink TSO super-segments written to the TUN device per second (`[tun] gso`). Each is one large TCP super-segment the kernel splits into MSS packets, so routing / nftables / conntrack / WireGuard run once per super-segment instead of per MSS. Rising under download means write-side TSO is active — this is the main CPU win. Zero with `gso = true` means the traffic is not coalescible (small / interactive) at that moment.
panels.title: Downlink TSO super-segments/s

panels.description: Uplink TCP GRO super-packets read from the TUN device per second (`[tun] gro`). Each is several inbound MSS segments the kernel coalesced into one >MSS packet, fed whole into the userspace TCP engine — the read-side offload win. Rises only under a bulk upload burst; near-zero on a light / ACK-only uplink is expected (nothing to coalesce). Compare against the downlink TSO rate to judge whether GRO earns its keep on this link.
panels.title: Uplink TCP GRO super-packets/s

panels.description: UDP GRO super-packets read from the TUN device per second. Expected to stay at 0: `TUN_F_USO4/6` is never requested, so the kernel re-segments UDP GRO back to datagrams before we read them and UDP stays per-datagram. A sustained non-zero value means a stale `TUN_F_USO` offload lingered on a persistent TUN interface from a previous run (recreate `ws0` to clear it, or restart with `gro = false` which now resets offload to 0). The read loop re-segments these back into datagrams, so UDP keeps working — but non-zero here is the signal to investigate stale offload.
panels.title: UDP GSO super-packets/s (expect 0)

panels.description: Downlink UDP USO super-segments written to the TUN device per second (`outcome="uso_supersegment"`). Each is one GSO_UDP_L4 aggregate of equal-sized datagrams the kernel splits per datagram — the write-side UDP offload from `[tun] uso`, aimed at bulk QUIC video. Non-zero means downlink UDP is coalescing; zero under heavy UDP means the source is not bursting equal-sized datagrams (or `uso` is off / unsupported).
panels.title: Downlink UDP USO super-segments/s

panels.description:
What BBR believes the last hop can carry (`estimate`) and how fast it is actually pouring into it (`pacing`, after the STARTUP/PROBE gain and both ceilings). Both gauges are sums over flows, so they are divided by the active-flow count here.

Read it against the real capacity of the last hop. An estimate far above what the link can do means BBR is over-reading the path and will pace into it until packets drop — the failure that capped the TUN downlink at ~5-10 Mbit (estimate sat at ~741 Mbit per flow). Estimate in the tens of Mbit on a Wi-Fi last hop is healthy.

`loss cap` is the loss-driven ceiling, averaged over the flows it is actually holding down (0 = no flow capped). Watch for it pinned near its 1 Mbit floor: that means the last hop keeps dropping and the cap cannot climb back.

targets.expr: label_replace(8 * sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_bbr_btlbw_bytes_per_second{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_flows_active{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

label_replace(8 * sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_bbr_pacing_rate_bytes_per_second{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_flows_active{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

label_replace(8 * sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_bbr_loss_cap_bytes_per_second{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_bbr_loss_capped_flows{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

panels.title: BBR bandwidth estimate vs pacing rate (avg per flow)

panels.description:
min-RTT is the best round-trip the path has shown (BBR's basis for the BDP); smoothed RTT is what it is answering in right now. The **gap between them is standing queue** — buffer we are filling ourselves.

Healthy: the two track each other. A smoothed RTT several times the min means bufferbloat on the last hop, and loss is usually already following. During the ~5-10 Mbit ceiling this ran 21 ms against a 2 ms min (12x); after the rate-sample fix it settled at ~2.8 ms against ~0.9 ms.

targets.expr: label_replace(sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_bbr_min_rtt_seconds{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_flows_active{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

label_replace(sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_smoothed_rtt_seconds{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_flows_active{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

panels.title: RTT: min vs smoothed (avg per flow) - queue we are building

panels.description:
Rate of loss episodes (fast-recovery entry or RTO) across TUN TCP flows. **The primary health signal for the downlink.**

Occasional blips are normal — BBR probes upward by design and a Wi-Fi last hop will drop the odd packet. A sustained rate means we are overrunning the last hop: each episode also halves the Reno window, and a window crushed by repeated loss becomes the real throughput limit regardless of what BBR thinks.
panels.title: BBR loss episodes/s

panels.description:
Flows whose BBR rate is currently throttled by the loss-driven cap: each loss episode multiplies the cap by 0.85, and loss-free rounds relax it back toward the estimate.

A few capped flows on a lossy hop are the mechanism working. Many flows pinned here — especially with the cap sitting near its 1 Mbit floor — means the last hop keeps dropping and the cap can never recover.
panels.title: Flows held down by the loss cap

panels.description: Bytes in flight per flow against the BBRv2 in-flight ceilings that bound it. The effective send window is `min(bbr_bdp_cap, inflight_hi, inflight_lo)` — a single BBRv2 model, no Reno window. `inflight_hi` is the long-term ceiling (where the path starts to overflow); `inflight_lo` is the short-term ceiling, backed off on a measured loss and released each probe. Both read 0 while unset (no loss over the 2% threshold yet). Pair with the RTT panel to see the queue being built.

targets.expr: label_replace(sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_inflight_bytes{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_flows_active{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

label_replace(sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_bbr_inflight_hi_bytes{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_flows_active{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

label_replace(sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_bbr_inflight_lo_bytes{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) / (sum by (instance, group, uplink) (outline_ws_tun_tcp_flows_active{job=~"$job",instance=~"$instance",group=~"$group"}) > 0), "instance_short", "$1", "instance", "^([^.]+).*")

panels.title: In-flight & BBRv2 ceilings (avg per flow)
